Formalised in lean-bombieri-vinogradov (in progress; the top-level theorem bombieri_vinogradov and the \Delta_\Lambda-intermediate BV_Delta_Lambda are stated but carry sorry; the Siegel–Walfisz theorem and the large sieve inequality are taken as axioms).
previews.label: «bombieri-vinogradov»
previews.title: Theorem 2.24

previews.html: One applies the Selberg sieve (Definition 2.25) to sift the interval (x, x+y] by primes up to z. The Selberg sieve gives an upper bound on the count of unsifted elements in terms of y / S(z) plus a remainder sum, where S(z) = \sum_{d \le z,\, d \mid \mathrm{rad}} \lambda_d^2 / \nu(d) is the Selberg bounding sum. A lower bound S(z) \ge \log(z)/2 follows from the primorial structure of the sieve support (each prime p \le z contributes 1/p to \nu). The remainder is bounded by 5z(1 + \log z)^3 using divisor-sum estimates, and combining the two gives the stated inequality. Non-sifted elements not accounted for by the sieve are at most z, adding the correction term. Converting the resulting bound on the number of primes in (x, x+y] into the stated \ll y/\log y form, and controlling the prime-counting tails, uses Chebyshev-type prime-counting estimates (Theorem 2.18).
previews.label: «brun-titchmarsh»
previews.title: Theorem 2.22

previews.html: (Brun–Titchmarsh inequality.) For any real x > 0, y > 0, and z > 1, the number of primes in the interval (x, x + y] satisfies \pi(x+y) - \pi(x) \;\le\; \frac{2y}{\log z} + 6z\,(1 + \log z)^3. In particular, taking z = \sqrt{y} gives \pi(x+y) - \pi(x) \ll y/\log y. Formalised in PrimeNumberTheoremAnd (sorry-free).
previews.label: «brun-titchmarsh»
previews.title: Theorem 2.22

previews.html:
Mathlib proves the more general statement that for any group G and nonempty finite A, B \subseteq G, one has \min(\mathrm{minOrder}(G),\; |A| + |B| - 1) \;\le\; |A + B|, where \mathrm{minOrder}(G) is the order of the smallest nontrivial subgroup of G. The \mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z} statement follows because the smallest nontrivial subgroup has order p — for p prime the additive group \mathbb{F}_p has no proper nontrivial subgroup, the same primality of the field \mathbb{F}_p that powers Fermat's little theorem (Theorem 1.10).

The general bound is proved by the e-transform method of DeVos. One picks a nonidentity element g such that A intersects its translate g + A, and replaces the pair (A, B) by either (A \cap (g + A),\; B \cup (-g + B)) or the complementary pair, both of which have the same |A| + |B| but strictly smaller sum-set or strictly smaller |A|. The induction terminates because the pair decreases at each step in a well-founded lexicographic order on (|A + B|,\; |A| + |B|,\; |A|).
previews.label: «cauchy-davenport»
previews.title: Theorem 10.2

previews.html: (Cauchy–Davenport, 1813/1935.) Let p be a prime and let A, B \subseteq \mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z} be nonempty. Then |A + B| \;\ge\; \min\!\bigl(p,\; |A| + |B| - 1\bigr).
previews.label: «cauchy-davenport»
previews.title: Theorem 10.2

previews.html: By the determinant formula (Theorem 9.15), consecutive convergents satisfy |C_n - C_{n+1}| = 1/(B_n B_{n+1}). Since the denominators B_n are bounded below by Fibonacci numbers, B_n \to \infty, and |v - C_n| \le 1/(B_n B_{n+1}) \to 0. An \varepsilon/N argument using the Archimedean property then gives the stated convergence.
previews.label: «cf-convergence»
previews.title: Theorem 9.16

previews.html: For any real number v, the sequence of convergents of the regular continued fraction expansion of v converges to v: \lim_{n \to \infty} C_n = v.
previews.label: «cf-convergence»
previews.title: Theorem 9.16

previews.html: By induction on n. The base case n = 0 is A_0 B_1 - B_0 A_1 = h \cdot (b_0 + h) - 1 \cdot \ldots which gives -1 = (-1)^1 after unfolding the recurrences (Definition 9.14). The inductive step uses the recurrence for continuants to factor out a b_n and apply the induction hypothesis, picking up a sign flip each step.
previews.label: «cf-determinant»
previews.title: Theorem 9.15

previews.html: For a simple continued fraction, the consecutive convergents satisfy the determinant formula A_n B_{n+1} - B_n A_{n+1} = (-1)^{n+1}. In particular, consecutive convergents C_n = A_n/B_n and C_{n+1} = A_{n+1}/B_{n+1} satisfy |C_n - C_{n+1}| = 1/(B_n B_{n+1}).
previews.label: «cf-determinant»
previews.title: Theorem 9.15

previews.html: The proof reduces to the abelian case via Chebotarev's crossing trick and does not use class field theory. Pick a representative \sigma \in C and let E = L^{\langle\sigma\rangle} be the fixed field (Theorem 4.3) of the cyclic subgroup generated by \sigma. Since L/E is abelian, the abelian Chebotarev theorem (proved by the cyclotomic crossing trick) gives \delta_E(\{\mathfrak{P} : \mathrm{Frob}_\mathfrak{P} = \sigma\}) = 1/|\mathrm{Gal}(L/E)|. For each prime \mathfrak{p} of K with Frobenius class C, exactly |G|/(f\,|C|) primes of L above \mathfrak{p} have Frobenius equal to \sigma (where f = \mathrm{ord}(\sigma)), by transitivity of the Galois action and the structure of the decomposition group (Definition 4.4). A density comparison between K and E using the asymptotic \sum N\mathfrak{p}^{-s} \sim \log(1/(s-1)) then gives \delta_K(C) = (f\,|C|/|G|) \cdot (1/f) = |C|/|G|.
previews.label: «chebotarev-density»
previews.title: Theorem 4.12

previews.html: (Chebotarev density theorem.) Let L/K be a finite Galois extension of number fields with Galois group G = \mathrm{Gal}(L/K) (Definition 4.1). For any conjugacy class C \subseteq G, the Dirichlet density (Definition 4.11) of the set of primes \mathfrak{p} of \mathcal{O}_K unramified in L whose Frobenius conjugacy class (Definition 4.8) equals C is \delta\!\left(\bigl\{\mathfrak{p} \subset \mathcal{O}_K : \mathrm{Frob}_{\mathfrak{p}} = C\bigr\}\right) = \frac{|C|}{|G|}. Formalised in chebotarev-density (sorry-free).
previews.label: «chebotarev-density»
previews.title: Theorem 4.12

previews.html: A prime \mathfrak{p} splits completely in L if and only if its Frobenius class is the trivial conjugacy class \{1\}. Applying Theorem 4.12 with C = \{1\} gives density 1/|G| = 1/[L:K], using |\mathrm{Gal}(L/K)| = [L:K] (Theorem 4.3).
previews.label: «chebotarev-split-completely»
previews.title: Theorem 4.13

previews.html: (Corollary.) For a finite Galois extension L/K of number fields (Definition 4.1), the Dirichlet density of primes of \mathcal{O}_K that split completely in L equals \delta\!\bigl(\{\mathfrak{p} : \mathfrak{p} \text{ splits completely in } L\}\bigr) = \frac{1}{[L:K]}. Formalised in chebotarev-density (sorry-free).
previews.label: «chebotarev-split-completely»
previews.title: Theorem 4.13

previews.html:
The upper bound \psi(x) \le (\log 4 + 4)x follows from a comparison with central binomial coefficients: \binom{2n}{n} \ge 4^n / (2n+1), so \prod_{n < p \le 2n} p \le \binom{2n}{n}, and the right-hand side is at most 4^n, giving the bound by induction on dyadic blocks.

The lower bound comes from the von Mangoldt convolution identity (Theorem 2.8): summing \sum_{d \mid n} \Lambda(d) = \log n over n \le x and comparing with bounds on \log\lfloor x \rfloor! via the Legendre valuation formula yields \psi(x) \ge x \log 2 - O(\sqrt{x}), establishing the lower bound.
previews.label: «chebyshev-bounds»
previews.title: Theorem 2.18

previews.html: (Chebyshev's bounds.) There exist positive constants c_1, c_2 such that for all x \ge 2, c_1 x \;\le\; \psi(x) \;\le\; c_2 x, where \psi(x) = \sum_{n \le x} \Lambda(n) is the Chebyshev \psi-function (Definition 2.7). One may take c_1 = 1 and c_2 = \log 4 + 4 in the formalization. Formalised in PrimeNumberTheoremAnd (sorry-free).
previews.label: «chebyshev-bounds»
previews.title: Theorem 2.18

previews.html:
(Chebyshev's lower bound on the primorial.) For every integer n \ge 1, the primorial — the product of all primes up to n — satisfies an exponential lower bound \prod_{p \le n} p \;\ge\; 2^{\,n} for n sufficiently large (more precisely, \vartheta(n) = \sum_{p \le n} \log p \ge cn for an explicit constant c > 0). This is a quantitative sharpening of the infinitude of primes (Theorem 1.1): not merely are there infinitely many primes, their product to n grows at least geometrically in n.

The proof bounds the central binomial coefficient \binom{2n}{n}, whose prime factorization (Theorem 1.3) is supported on primes \le 2n, between 2^n and the primorial of 2n. PR #37299 supplies this lower bound, a standard ingredient of Chebyshev-type prime-counting estimates and of Bertrand's postulate. In review — mathlib PR #37299.
previews.label: «chebyshev-primorial»
previews.title: Theorem 1.29

previews.html: The map sending x \bmod mn to the pair (x \bmod m,\ x \bmod n) is a ring homomorphism. Its kernel consists of residues divisible by both m and n; as \gcd(m,n) = 1, this means divisible by mn, so the map is injective. Both sides have mn elements, hence it is a bijection, and therefore a ring isomorphism.
previews.label: «chinese-remainder»
previews.title: Theorem 1.14

previews.html: (Chinese remainder theorem.) If m and n are coprime, then reduction modulo m and modulo n gives a ring isomorphism \mathbb{Z}/mn\mathbb{Z} \;\cong\; \mathbb{Z}/m\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}. In particular, any pair of congruences x \equiv a \pmod{m} and x \equiv b \pmod{n} has a solution, unique modulo mn.
previews.label: «chinese-remainder»
previews.title: Theorem 1.14

previews.html: The proof bounds the number of ideal classes (Definition 3.6) using Minkowski's geometry-of-numbers bound (Definition 3.9): every ideal class contains an ideal of norm at most the Minkowski bound M_K = \left(\frac{4}{\pi}\right)^{r_2} \frac{n!}{n^n} \sqrt{|\mathrm{disc}(K)|}, where n = [K:\mathbb{Q}] and r_2 is the number of pairs of complex embeddings. Since there are only finitely many ideals of bounded norm in \mathcal{O}_K, the class group is represented by finitely many ideal classes.
previews.label: «class-group-finite»
previews.title: Theorem 3.7

previews.html: The class group \mathrm{Cl}(K) of any number field K is finite.
previews.label: «class-group-finite»
previews.title: Theorem 3.7

previews.html: The class group \mathrm{Cl}(K) of a Dedekind domain R (Definition 3.3) (e.g. R = \mathcal{O}_K) is the quotient of the group of invertible fractional ideals by its subgroup of principal fractional ideals: \mathrm{Cl}(K) = \{\text{invertible fractional ideals of } \mathcal{O}_K\} \;/\; \{\alpha \mathcal{O}_K : \alpha \in K^\times\}. The class group is trivial if and only if \mathcal{O}_K is a principal ideal domain.
previews.label: «class-group»
previews.title: Definition 3.6

previews.html: Because \mathcal{O}_K is faithfully flat over \mathcal{O}_{K^+}, extension followed by contraction of ideals is the identity, so an ideal of \mathcal{O}_{K^+} that becomes principal in \mathcal{O}_K was already principal: if I\mathcal{O}_K = (a), then I\mathcal{O}_K is fixed by complex conjugation, whence \bar a = u a for a unit u with u\bar u = 1; such a unit is \pm \zeta_p^m, and adjusting a by a power of \zeta_p produces a conjugation-fixed generator descending to \mathcal{O}_{K^+}. Thus the class-group map is injective, and Lagrange's theorem for the finite class groups (Theorem 3.7) gives h^+ \mid h.
previews.label: «class-number-splitting»
previews.title: Theorem 8.18

previews.html: Let p be an odd prime, K = \mathbb{Q}(\zeta_p) the p-th cyclotomic field (Definition 3.14), and K^+ its maximal real subfield. Writing h = h(K) and h^+ = h(K^+) for the two class numbers (Definition 3.6), the inclusion \mathcal{O}_{K^+} \subseteq \mathcal{O}_K induces an injection of class groups, so h^+ \mid h. The quotient h^- = h / h^+ is the relative class number. Formalised in KummerCriterion and flt-regular-bernoulli (sorry-free).
previews.label: «class-number-splitting»
previews.title: Theorem 8.18

previews.html:
(The completion of \mathbb{Q} at a finite place is \mathbb{Q}_p.) By Ostrowski's theorem (Theorem 5.14) the nontrivial places of \mathbb{Q} are the archimedean place and one finite place for each prime p, the latter given by the p-adic absolute value (Definition 5.5). For each prime p, the completion of \mathbb{Q} at the finite place p — the completion of \mathbb{Q} with respect to the p-adic absolute value, formed as a valued field via its adic valuation — is canonically isomorphic, as a valued (indeed topological) field, to the field of p-adic numbers: \widehat{\mathbb{Q}}^{(p)} \;\cong\; \mathbb{Q}_p. The isomorphism is compatible with the valuations and carries the completion of \mathbb{Z}_{(p)} onto \mathbb{Z}_p (Definition 5.7).

PR #21950 identifies the abstract place-completion of \mathbb{Q} with the concrete \mathbb{Q}_p (Definition 5.6), reconciling the adic-valuation construction with mathlib's Padic. This is the bridge that lets number-field place-completions specialise correctly to \mathbb{Q}. In review — mathlib PR #21950.
previews.label: «completion-rat-padic»
previews.title: Theorem 5.24

previews.html:
Let A be a topological ring and v : A \to \Gamma_0 a valuation (Definition 5.1). We say v is continuous if for every \gamma \in \Gamma_0 the set \{ a \in A : v(a) < \gamma \} is open in A. The set of continuous points of \mathrm{Spv}(A) (Definition 5.17) is denoted \mathrm{Cont}(A) \subseteq \mathrm{Spv}(A).

For a topological ring with the discrete topology every valuation is continuous; for a non-archimedean field the p-adic absolute value is continuous in this sense. Formalised in Adic-Spaces (sorry-free); see Valuation.IsContinuous, Cont.
previews.label: «continuous-valuation»
previews.title: Definition 5.18

previews.html: For k < 12, the space S_k(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})) is zero.
previews.label: «cusp-form-rank-zero-lt-twelve»
previews.title: Lemma 6.21

previews.html: The inclusion S_k(\Gamma) \hookrightarrow M_k(\Gamma) is the linear map sending a cusp form to the same function viewed as a modular form. It is injective because a modular form is a cusp form if and only if it vanishes at all cusps (Definition 6.6). The linear equivalence follows from the fact that the range of an injective linear map is linearly equivalent to the domain.
previews.label: «cusp-form-submodule»
previews.title: Lemma 6.7

previews.html: The space of cusp forms S_k(\Gamma) embeds into M_k(\Gamma) as a \mathbb{C}-submodule, and the type S_k(\Gamma) is linearly equivalent to this submodule: S_k(\Gamma) \;\simeq_{\mathbb{C}}\; \mathrm{cuspFormSubmodule}(\Gamma, k) \;\subseteq\; M_k(\Gamma).
previews.label: «cusp-form-submodule»
previews.title: Lemma 6.7

previews.html: This is proved by establishing the linear equivalence S_k \cong M_{k-12} given by dividing by \Delta (Theorem 6.19). At k = 12, M_0 is spanned by the constant function 1, so S_{12} is at most one-dimensional. The discriminant itself (Definition 6.16) shows it is at least one-dimensional.
previews.label: «cusp-form-weight-twelve-rank-one»
previews.title: Lemma 6.18

previews.html: The space S_{12}(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})) is one-dimensional over \mathbb{C}.
previews.label: «cusp-form-weight-twelve-rank-one»
previews.title: Lemma 6.18

previews.html: A cusp form of weight k and level \Gamma is a slash-invariant form (Definition 6.4) that is holomorphic on \mathbb{H} and vanishes at every cusp: the same f \mid_k g tends to 0 as \mathrm{Im}(z) \to \infty. The subspace of cusp forms is denoted S_k(\Gamma) \subseteq M_k(\Gamma).
previews.label: «cusp-form»
previews.title: Definition 6.6

previews.html: Let S be a set of positive integers and A \subseteq B a ring extension. We say that B is an S-cyclotomic extension of A (and write \mathrm{IsCyclotomicExtension}(S, A, B)) if for every nonzero n \in S there exists a primitive n-th root of unity \zeta_n \in B, and B is generated over A by all n-th roots of unity for n \in S. For a single integer n, the n-th cyclotomic field over \mathbb{Q} is \mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n), the splitting field of the cyclotomic polynomial \Phi_n.
previews.label: «cyclotomic-extension»
previews.title: Definition 3.14

previews.html: The minimal polynomial of \zeta_n over \mathbb{Q} is the n-th cyclotomic polynomial \Phi_n(X), whose degree is the value \varphi(n) of Euler's totient (Definition 1.5) — the number of primitive n-th roots of unity. When \Phi_n is irreducible over K — in particular for K = \mathbb{Q} by Gauss's classical argument — the extension L = K(\zeta_n) satisfies [L:K] = \deg \Phi_n = \varphi(n). The proof uses the power basis generated by a primitive n-th root of unity together with the fact that its minimal polynomial is \Phi_n (Definition 3.14).
previews.label: «cyclotomic-finrank»
previews.title: Lemma 3.15

previews.html: The cyclotomic field \mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n) has degree \varphi(n) = [\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n):\mathbb{Q}] over \mathbb{Q}, where \varphi is Euler's totient function.
previews.label: «cyclotomic-finrank»
previews.title: Lemma 3.15

previews.html:
(Inertia field of a cyclotomic field.) Let n = p^k m with p \nmid m, and let K = \mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n) be the n-th cyclotomic field (Definition 3.14). The inertia field of the prime p in K — the largest subextension in which p is unramified — is \mathbb{Q}(\zeta_m), and the ramification index of p in K is e = \varphi(p^k) (Definition 3.11), while its inertia degree (Definition 3.12) is the multiplicative order of p modulo m. Equivalently, p is totally ramified in \mathbb{Q}(\zeta_{p^k})/\mathbb{Q} and unramified in \mathbb{Q}(\zeta_m)/\mathbb{Q}, and the two extensions are linearly disjoint over \mathbb{Q}.

The PR computes the inertia field explicitly inside the cyclotomic tower, giving the splitting of a rational prime p in \mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n) in terms of p \bmod m. In review — mathlib PR #37031.
previews.label: «cyclotomic-inertia-field»
previews.title: Theorem 3.20

previews.html: Since \zeta_p = 1 - \lambda_p one has \mathbb{Z}[\zeta_p] = \mathbb{Z}[\lambda_p]. The discriminant of the power basis equals (-1)^{(p-1)/2} N_{K/\mathbb{Q}}(\Phi_p'(\zeta_p)), and the explicit value \Phi_p'(\zeta_p) = -p\zeta_p^{p-1}/\lambda_p together with N_{K/\mathbb{Q}}(\lambda_p) = p gives p^{p-2} up to sign. As this discriminant is a power of p and the minimal polynomial \Phi_p is Eisenstein at p, an integrality argument forces any x \in \mathcal{O}_K into \mathbb{Z}[\zeta_p]; the reverse inclusion is clear.
previews.label: «cyclotomic-ring-of-integers»
previews.title: Theorem 8.12

previews.html: Let p be an odd prime, \zeta_p a primitive p-th root of unity, and \lambda_p = 1 - \zeta_p. Then the ring of integers (Definition 3.2) of K = \mathbb{Q}(\zeta_p) is \mathcal{O}_K = \mathbb{Z}[\zeta_p] = \mathbb{Z}[\lambda_p], and the discriminant of the power basis \{1, \zeta_p, \ldots, \zeta_p^{p-2}\} is (-1)^{(p-1)/2} p^{\,p-2}. Formalised in flt-regular (sorry-free).
previews.label: «cyclotomic-ring-of-integers»
previews.title: Theorem 8.12

previews.html: The hypothesis is equivalent to non-vanishing of the determinant of the Kummer logarithm matrix, whose p-adic-logarithm entries factor as a diagonal of Bernoulli factors times a Teichmüller–Vandermonde matrix; the Vandermonde determinant is a unit, so the determinant is nonzero precisely when every Bernoulli diagonal factor — that is, every \operatorname{num}(B_{2k}) mod p — is nonzero. A nonzero determinant forces C^+ to be p-saturated in \mathcal{O}_{K^+}^\times (every plus-side unit that is a p-th power already has all its cyclotomic-unit exponents divisible by p), and a p-saturated finite-index subgroup containing the ambient p-torsion has index prime to p.
previews.label: «cyclotomic-units-saturation»
previews.title: Theorem 8.21

previews.html: Let p be an odd prime and let C^+ \le \mathcal{O}_{K^+}^\times be the subgroup generated by -1 and the real cyclotomic units (1 - \zeta_p^a)/(1 - \zeta_p) of K^+ = \mathbb{Q}(\zeta_p)^+. If p \nmid \operatorname{num}(B_{2k}) for every k with 1 \le k and 2k \le p - 3 (Definition 8.8), then p \nmid [\,\mathcal{O}_{K^+}^\times : C^+\,]. Formalised in KummerCriterion and flt-regular-bernoulli (sorry-free).
previews.label: «cyclotomic-units-saturation»
previews.title: Theorem 8.21

previews.html: A Dedekind domain is a Noetherian integrally closed integral domain in which every nonzero prime ideal is maximal. Equivalently, a Dedekind domain is an integral domain in which every nonzero ideal factors uniquely as a product of prime ideals.
previews.label: «dedekind-domain»
previews.title: Definition 3.3

previews.html: The proof proceeds by strong induction on k. The base cases k \le 11 are handled directly (using the vanishing of S_k for k < 12 (Lemma 6.21) and the rank-one result for weights 4 and 6). For k \ge 12, the isomorphism S_k \cong M_{k-12} (Theorem 6.19) and the splitting \dim M_k = 1 + \dim S_k (Lemma 6.22) reduce the formula to the inductive hypothesis. The parity condition on k \mod 12 is then verified by arithmetic.
previews.label: «dimension-level-one»
previews.title: Theorem 6.20

previews.html: For every even natural number k, the dimension of M_k(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})) over \mathbb{C} is \dim_{\mathbb{C}} M_k(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})) \;=\; \begin{cases} \lfloor k/12 \rfloor & \text{if } k \equiv 2 \pmod{12}, \\ \lfloor k/12 \rfloor + 1 & \text{otherwise.} \end{cases} In particular M_0 = \mathbb{C}, M_2 = 0, M_4 \cong \mathbb{C}, and M_6 \cong \mathbb{C}.
previews.label: «dimension-level-one»
previews.title: Theorem 6.20

previews.html: Partition the unit interval [0, 1) into n+1 subintervals of length 1/(n+1). Consider the n+2 numbers \{m\xi\} for m = 0, 1, \ldots, n, where \{t\} denotes the fractional part. By pigeonhole, two of these lie in the same subinterval: |\{k\xi\} - \{k'\xi\}| < 1/(n+1). Setting k = |k - k'| and j = \lfloor k\xi \rfloor or the nearest integer gives the inequality.
previews.label: «dirichlet-approx»
previews.title: Theorem 9.17

previews.html: (Dirichlet's approximation theorem.) For any real number \xi and any positive integer n, there exist integers j and k with 0 < k \le n such that |k\xi - j| \le \frac{1}{n+1}. Equivalently, there is a rational j/k with |\xi - j/k| \le 1/(k(n+1)).
previews.label: «dirichlet-approx»
previews.title: Theorem 9.17

previews.html: A Dirichlet character of modulus q is a completely multiplicative function \chi : \mathbb{Z}/q\mathbb{Z} \to R that is a multiplicative character of the monoid \mathbb{Z}/q\mathbb{Z} — that is, a monoid homomorphism from \mathbb{Z}/q\mathbb{Z} to the multiplicative monoid of R that sends non-units to 0. In the analytic theory one takes R = \mathbb{C}.
previews.label: «dirichlet-character»
previews.title: Definition 2.3

previews.html: Specialise Theorem 4.12 to K = \mathbb{Q} and the cyclotomic extension L = \mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n) (Definition 3.14). The cyclotomic character identifies \mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)/\mathbb{Q}) with (\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^\times; the Frobenius at an unramified prime p corresponds to p \bmod n. Since G is abelian every conjugacy class is a singleton, so the Chebotarev density at \{a\} is 1/|(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^\times| = 1/\varphi(n).
previews.label: «dirichlet-density-ap»
previews.title: Theorem 4.14

previews.html: (Dirichlet, Chebotarev density form.) For n \ge 1 and a \in (\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^\times, the Dirichlet density (Definition 4.11) of rational primes p \equiv a \pmod n is \delta\!\bigl(\{p \text{ prime} : p \equiv a \pmod n\}\bigr) = \frac{1}{\varphi(n)}. Formalised in chebotarev-density (sorry-free).
previews.label: «dirichlet-density-ap»
previews.title: Theorem 4.14

previews.html: Let K be a number field with ring of integers \mathcal{O}_K. A set S of nonzero prime ideals of \mathcal{O}_K has Dirichlet density \delta \in \mathbb{R}, written \delta(S) = \delta, if \lim_{s \to 1^+} \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{\mathfrak{p} \in S} N\mathfrak{p}^{-s}} {\displaystyle\sum_{\mathfrak{p}} N\mathfrak{p}^{-s}} = \delta, where the denominator runs over all nonzero prime ideals of \mathcal{O}_K and N\mathfrak{p} = [\mathcal{O}_K : \mathfrak{p}] is the absolute norm. The denominator is asymptotic to \log(1/(s-1)) as s \downarrow 1.
previews.label: «dirichlet-density»
previews.title: Definition 4.11

previews.html: The proof is the same as for \zeta (Theorem 2.2): the complete multiplicativity of \chi (Definition 2.3) allows the absolutely convergent Dirichlet series (Definition 2.4) to be re-expanded as an Euler product, with each local factor \sum_{k \ge 0} \chi(p^k) p^{-ks} = \sum_{k \ge 0} \chi(p)^k p^{-ks} = (1 - \chi(p) p^{-s})^{-1} converging geometrically.
previews.label: «dirichlet-lfunction-euler-product»
previews.title: Theorem 2.6

previews.html: (Euler product for L(s, \chi).) For \operatorname{Re}(s) > 1, L(s, \chi) = \prod_{p} \frac{1}{1 - \chi(p)\, p^{-s}}, where the product converges absolutely.
previews.label: «dirichlet-lfunction-euler-product»
previews.title: Theorem 2.6

previews.html:
The proof splits into two cases. If \chi^2 \ne 1 or t \ne 0, a trigonometric positivity argument applied to |L(1,s)^3 L(\chi,s)^4 L(\chi^2,s)| shows the product is bounded below by 1 for \operatorname{Re}(s) > 1; a zero at 1 + it would force this product to zero as \operatorname{Re}(s) \to 1^+, a contradiction.

For a quadratic character \chi^2 = 1 with t = 0, assume for contradiction L(\chi,1) = 0. Define the entire function F(s) = \zeta(s) L(\chi, s) (the simple zero of L(\chi, s) at s = 1 cancels the pole of \zeta). For \operatorname{Re}(s) > 1, F agrees with the L-series of the Dirichlet convolution \mathbf{1} * \chi, which takes non-negative values. A classical lemma relating the sign of iterated derivatives to coefficient positivity shows (-1)^m F^{(m)}(2) \ge 0 for all m \ge 0, forcing F(x) > 0 for all real x \le 2. But the trivial zero of \zeta gives F(-2) = \zeta(-2) L(\chi,-2) = 0, a contradiction (Theorem 2.10).
previews.label: «dirichlet-lfunction-nonvanishing-line»
previews.title: Theorem 2.17

previews.html: (Non-vanishing on \operatorname{Re}(s) = 1.) Let \chi be a Dirichlet character (Definition 2.3) and t \in \mathbb{R}. If \chi \ne 1 or t \ne 0, then L(\chi,\, 1 + it) \;\ne\; 0. In particular, for every non-trivial \chi the Dirichlet L-function (Definition 2.5) has no zero on the entire line \operatorname{Re}(s) = 1. Formalised in DirichletNonvanishing (sorry-free).
previews.label: «dirichlet-lfunction-nonvanishing-line»
previews.title: Theorem 2.17

previews.html: For a Dirichlet character \chi of modulus q (Definition 2.3), the Dirichlet L-function L(s, \chi) is the meromorphic continuation to all of \mathbb{C} of the L-series (Definition 2.4) of \chi, L(s, \chi) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{\chi(n)}{n^s}, \qquad \operatorname{Re}(s) > 1. When \chi is non-trivial the continuation is entire; when \chi is the trivial character of modulus q the function has a simple pole at s = 1 and agrees (up to finitely many Euler factors) with \zeta(s).
previews.label: «dirichlet-lfunction»
previews.title: Definition 2.5

previews.html:
The logarithmic embedding sends a unit u to the vector (\log|u|_v)_{v} indexed by the infinite places v of K, with the multiplicity 1 for real places and 2 for complex places. Dirichlet's theorem asserts that the image of \mathcal{O}_K^\times under this map is a full-rank lattice in the trace-zero hyperplane of \mathbb{R}^{r_1 + r_2} (a real vector space of dimension r_1 + r_2 - 1 = r).

The kernel of the logarithmic embedding is the set of units of absolute value 1 at every infinite place, i.e. the roots of unity in \mathcal{O}_K. That this group is finite follows from Definition 3.2 (it is a finite set by a norm bound). That the image is a full lattice requires showing it is both discrete (using Minkowski's bound, Definition 3.9) and spans all of \mathbb{R}^r (a subtler geometric argument constructing units with prescribed archimedean absolute values). Together these yield the claimed direct product decomposition.
previews.label: «dirichlet-unit-theorem»
previews.title: Theorem 3.10

previews.html: (Dirichlet's unit theorem.) Let K be a number field with r_1 real embeddings and r_2 pairs of complex embeddings, and let r = r_1 + r_2 - 1. Every unit u \in \mathcal{O}_K^\times can be written uniquely as u = \zeta \cdot \varepsilon_1^{a_1} \cdots \varepsilon_r^{a_r}, where \zeta is a root of unity in \mathcal{O}_K, the \varepsilon_i form a fundamental system of units, and a_i \in \mathbb{Z}. Equivalently, \mathcal{O}_K^\times \cong \mu(K) \times \mathbb{Z}^r, where \mu(K) is the (finite cyclic) group of roots of unity in K.
previews.label: «dirichlet-unit-theorem»
previews.title: Theorem 3.10

previews.html: By Dirichlet's theorem (Theorem 2.11) the residue class a \bmod q contains infinitely many primes. An infinite set of primes is unbounded, so for any threshold n at least one of them exceeds n; this is the asserted prime p > n with p \equiv a \pmod q.
previews.label: «dirichlets-theorem-gt»
previews.title: Corollary 2.12

previews.html: Let q be a positive integer, a a natural number coprime to q, and n any natural number. Then there exists a prime p > n with p \equiv a \pmod{q}.
previews.label: «dirichlets-theorem-gt»
previews.title: Corollary 2.12

previews.html:
The proof uses the analytic properties of Dirichlet L-functions (Definition 2.5). For each Dirichlet character \chi of modulus q (Definition 2.3) one forms the logarithmic sum \sum_p \chi(p) p^{-s}. Via the Euler product (Theorem 2.6) this is asymptotically -\log L(s, \chi) plus a convergent error as s \to 1^+.

Orthogonality of characters then isolates the contribution from the residue class a: the sum \sum_{p \equiv a \pmod{q}} p^{-s} equals \frac{1}{\varphi(q)} \sum_\chi \overline{\chi}(a) (-\log L(s, \chi) + \text{convergent}). The trivial character contributes a \log\frac{1}{s-1} term (from the pole of \zeta), while the non-trivial characters contribute finite limits at s = 1 because L(1, \chi) \ne 0 (Theorem 2.10). Hence the full sum diverges as s \to 1^+, proving the residue class contains infinitely many primes.
previews.label: «dirichlets-theorem»
previews.title: Theorem 2.11

previews.html: (Dirichlet's theorem.) Let q be a positive integer and let a be an integer coprime to q. Then there are infinitely many primes p with p \equiv a \pmod{q}. In particular, every residue class coprime to the modulus contains infinitely many primes.
previews.label: «dirichlets-theorem»
previews.title: Theorem 2.11

previews.html: The combination E_4^3 - E_6^2 is a cusp form of weight 12 (Definition 6.6): its constant q-expansion coefficient vanishes because the constant terms of E_4 and E_6 are both 1 (Lemma 6.14). Since S_{12}(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})) is one-dimensional (Lemma 6.18), this combination is a scalar multiple of \Delta. Reading off the first q-expansion coefficient on each side — (E_4^3 - E_6^2) has coefficient 1728 at q^1 while \Delta has coefficient 1 (Lemma 6.23) — pins the scalar to 1728.
previews.label: «discriminant-e4-e6»
previews.title: Theorem 6.17

previews.html: The modular discriminant satisfies the identity \Delta(z) \;=\; \frac{E_4(z)^3 - E_6(z)^2}{1728} pointwise for all z \in \mathbb{H}.
previews.label: «discriminant-e4-e6»
previews.title: Theorem 6.17

previews.html: Since \Delta is everywhere nonzero on \mathbb{H} (it is a nonzero eta-product), dividing a cusp form of weight k by \Delta produces a holomorphic function on \mathbb{H}, and the slash-invariance passes to weight k - 12 (Definition 6.3). Boundedness at the cusps follows from the fact that the zero of a cusp form at a cusp is at least as deep as the zero of \Delta. The inverse map multiplies by \Delta.
previews.label: «discriminant-equiv»
previews.title: Theorem 6.19

previews.html: For any weight k, multiplication by the discriminant \Delta defines a linear isomorphism S_k(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\; M_{k-12}(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})), \quad f \;\mapsto\; f / \Delta.
previews.label: «discriminant-equiv»
previews.title: Theorem 6.19

previews.html: The first q-expansion coefficient of \Delta is 1: \Delta(z) \;=\; q \,+\, \sum_{n=2}^{\infty} \tau(n) \, q^n, \quad q = e^{2\pi i z}, where \tau is the Ramanujan tau function.
previews.label: «discriminant-q-coeff-one»
previews.title: Lemma 6.23

previews.html: The x-coordinate companion polynomial \Phi_n \in R[X] is \Phi_n = X \cdot \Psi\!\mathrm{Sq}_n - \mathrm{pre}\Psi_{n+1} \cdot \mathrm{pre}\Psi_{n-1} \cdot \begin{cases} 1 & n \text{ even,} \\ \Psi_2^{\,2} & n \text{ odd,} \end{cases} where \Psi\!\mathrm{Sq}_n = \mathrm{pre}\Psi_n^2 \cdot \Psi_2^{\,2} (even) or \mathrm{pre}\Psi_n^2 (odd). In the coordinate ring, \Phi_n coincides with \phi_n (Definition 7.14).
previews.label: «division-polynomial-Phi»
previews.title: Definition 7.15

previews.html: The normalised companion sequence \Psi_n \in R[X, Y] is defined from the univariate polynomials \mathrm{pre}\Psi_n \in R[X] by \Psi_n = \begin{cases} \mathrm{pre}\Psi_n \cdot \psi_2 & n \text{ even,} \\ \mathrm{pre}\Psi_n & n \text{ odd.} \end{cases} The sequence \mathrm{pre}\Psi_n satisfies the same EDS recurrence as \psi_n but with the auxiliary parameter \Psi_2^{\,2} = 4X^3 + b_2 X^2 + 2b_4 X + b_6 substituted for \psi_2^2. In the coordinate ring R[E], \psi_n and \Psi_n coincide (Definition 7.13).
previews.label: «division-polynomial-Psi»
previews.title: Definition 7.14

previews.html: For an integer n, the n-th division polynomial \psi_n \in R[X, Y] of a Weierstrass curve W over R (Definition 7.1) — whose auxiliary quantities b_2, b_4, b_6, b_8 enter the initial terms below — is defined by the recurrence of a normalised elliptic divisibility sequence with initial values \psi_0 = 0,\quad \psi_1 = 1,\quad \psi_2 = 2Y + a_1 X + a_3, \psi_3 = 3X^4 + b_2 X^3 + 3b_4 X^2 + 3b_6 X + b_8, and \psi_4 = \psi_2 \cdot (2X^6 + b_2 X^5 + 5b_4 X^4 + 10b_6 X^3 + 10b_8 X^2 + (b_2 b_8 - b_4 b_6) X + (b_4 b_8 - b_6^2)). The associated polynomials \Phi_n \in R[X] and \phi_n \in R[X, Y] are defined by \Phi_n = X \Psi_n^2 - \psi_{n+1} \psi_{n-1},\quad \phi_n = ({\psi_{2n}}/{\psi_n} - \psi_n(a_1 \Phi_n + a_3 \Psi_n^2)) / 2, where \Psi_n is a companion sequence congruent to \psi_n modulo the Weierstrass polynomial.
previews.label: «division-polynomial-psi»
previews.title: Definition 7.13

previews.html:
(E_4 and E_6 generate the graded ring of level-one modular forms, freely.) The graded \mathbb{C}-algebra of modular forms of level \mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z}), M_*(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})) = \bigoplus_{k} M_k(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})), is a free polynomial algebra on the two Eisenstein series E_4 and E_6 (Definition 6.11): M_*(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})) \;=\; \mathbb{C}[E_4, E_6], with E_4, E_6 algebraically independent. Concretely, the monomials E_4^a E_6^b with 4a + 6b = k form a \mathbb{C}-basis of M_k(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})) for every even k \ge 0.

PR #38813 proves both that E_4, E_6 generate and that they are free (no algebraic relations), upgrading the numerical dimension formula (Theorem 6.20) to an explicit ring presentation. Freeness is equivalent to the discriminant identity \Delta = (E_4^3 - E_6^2)/1728 (Theorem 6.17) cutting out the cusp ideal. In review — mathlib PR #38813.
previews.label: «e4-e6-graded-ring»
previews.title: Theorem 6.36

previews.html:
Eigenforms and normalised newforms. An eigenform of weight k and level \Gamma_1(N) is a cusp form f carrying a Nebentypus character \chi (so f \in S_k(N,\chi)$) that is a simultaneous eigenfunction of all Hecke operators T(n) with (n,N) = 1: for each such n there is a scalar \lambda_n \in \mathbb{C} with T(n) f = \lambda_n f. The old subspace S_k^\flat(N) is the span of all level-raising images f(\ell z) from proper divisors of N; the new subspace S_k^\sharp(N) is its Petersson-orthogonal complement. A newform (normalised) is an eigenform in S_k^\sharp(N) normalised so that a_1(f) = 1. Formalised in LeanModularForms on branch hecke-ring (sorry-free).

Depends on: Definition 6.6 Definition 6.26
previews.label: «eigenform-newform»
previews.title: Definition 6.29

previews.html: Applying T(n) to the normalised form f gives T(n) f = \lambda_n f; reading off the first Fourier coefficient and using the explicit Fourier-coefficient formula for T(n) f (Definition 6.26) — which at index 1 collapses the divisor sum to the single term d = 1, yielding a_1(T(n)f) = a_n(f) — together with the eigenvalue equation a_1(\lambda_n f) = \lambda_n a_1(f) = \lambda_n (since a_1(f) = 1) gives \lambda_n = a_n(f). Multiplicativity of the Fourier coefficients for coprime indices then follows from T(mn) = T(m) T(n) (Theorem 6.28).
previews.label: «eigenvalue-equals-fourier-coeff»
previews.title: Theorem 6.30

previews.html:
Eigenvalue equals Fourier coefficient for a normalised newform. Let f be a normalised newform of weight k and level \Gamma_1(N) with Nebentypus character \chi$. For every n \ge 1 coprime to N, the Hecke eigenvalue at n equals the n$-th q$-expansion coefficient: \lambda_n(f) \;=\; a_n(f). In particular the Fourier coefficients of a newform are multiplicative: a_{mn}(f) = a_m(f) a_n(f) for \gcd(m,n) = 1. Formalised in LeanModularForms on branch hecke-ring (sorry-free).

Depends on: Definition 6.29 Definition 6.8
previews.label: «eigenvalue-equals-fourier-coeff»
previews.title: Theorem 6.30

previews.html: The slash-invariance of the Eisenstein series (Definition 6.11) under \Gamma(N) follows from the fact that the set of coprime pairs congruent to a mod N is preserved under the row-vector action of \Gamma(N) (Definition 6.4). Holomorphicity on \mathbb{H} comes from the uniform convergence of the defining series on compact subsets of \mathbb{H}, which in turn follows from the estimate |mz + n|^{-k} \le C \cdot \mathrm{Im}(z)^{-k/2} on compact sets. Boundedness at the cusps uses the explicit analysis of the cusp-function transform (Definition 6.8).
previews.label: «eisenstein-is-modular-form»
previews.title: Theorem 6.12

previews.html: For any k \ge 3, N \ge 1, and a \in (\mathbb{Z}/N\mathbb{Z})^2, the Eisenstein series \mathcal{E}_{k,a} defines a modular form in M_k(\Gamma(N)).
previews.label: «eisenstein-is-modular-form»
previews.title: Theorem 6.12

previews.html: The constant term of the q-expansion of E_k is 1 (Lemma 6.14). In particular E_k is not identically zero.
previews.label: «eisenstein-nonzero»
previews.title: Theorem 6.13

previews.html: For every even k \ge 3, the normalised Eisenstein series E_k is nonzero.
previews.label: «eisenstein-nonzero»
previews.title: Theorem 6.13

previews.html: For even k \ge 3, the constant term of the q-expansion of E_k is a_0(E_k) \;=\; 1.
previews.label: «eisenstein-q-expansion-coeff-zero»
previews.title: Lemma 6.14

previews.html: The key identity is the Lipschitz formula \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} (z + n)^{-k} \;=\; \frac{(-2\pi i)^k}{(k-1)!} \sum_{m=1}^{\infty} m^{k-1} e^{2\pi i m z}, which converts the defining sum of the normalised Eisenstein series (Definition 6.11) over coprime pairs into a sum of divisor-power sums. Summing instead over all nonzero integer pairs factors as 2\zeta(k) (Definition 2.1) times the coprime sum; isolating the coprime contribution by Möbius inversion (Theorem 1.17) over the gcd then yields the coefficient formula. The stated form follows from the value \zeta(k) = -(2\pi i)^k B_k / (2 \cdot k!).
previews.label: «eisenstein-q-expansion»
previews.title: Lemma 6.15

previews.html: For even k \ge 3 and every m \ge 1, the m-th q-expansion coefficient of E_k is a_m(E_k) \;=\; -\frac{2k}{B_k} \, \sigma_{k-1}(m), where B_k is the k-th Bernoulli number and \sigma_{k-1}(m) = \sum_{d \mid m} d^{k-1} is the divisor-power sum.
previews.label: «eisenstein-q-expansion»
previews.title: Lemma 6.15

previews.html: For an integer k \ge 3, a positive integer N, and a congruence condition a \in (\mathbb{Z}/N\mathbb{Z})^2, the Eisenstein series of weight k, level \Gamma(N), and characteristic a is the function \mathcal{E}_{k,a}(z) \;=\; \sum_{\substack{(m,n) \in \mathbb{Z}^2,\; \gcd(m,n) = 1 \\ (m,n) \equiv a \pmod{N}}} \frac{1}{(mz + n)^k}. The normalised Eisenstein series of weight k and level 1 is E_k(z) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \sum_{\substack{(m,n) \in \mathbb{Z}^2 \\ \gcd(m,n) = 1}} \frac{1}{(mz + n)^k}.
previews.label: «eisenstein-series»
previews.title: Definition 6.11

previews.html:
(Characterisation of elliptic divisibility sequences.) An elliptic divisibility sequence (EDS) is a sequence (W_n)_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} in a commutative ring satisfying W_0 = 0, W_1 = 1, and the recurrence W_{m+n}\,W_{m-n}\,W_1^2 \;=\; W_{m+1}\,W_{m-1}\,W_n^2 \;-\; W_{n+1}\,W_{n-1}\,W_m^2 \quad (m \ge n). The PR proves that the full elliptic recurrence is equivalent to the pair of even/odd duplication recurrences (in W_{2n+1} and W_{2n}), so an EDS is determined by its initial values together with these two recursions. The division polynomials \psi_n of a Weierstrass curve (Definition 7.13) form the universal EDS, and evaluating an EDS along a point records the denominators of its multiples on the curve (Theorem 7.12).

PR #13057 characterises EDS via the even-odd recursion, the algebraic backbone for working with division polynomials and torsion on elliptic curves. In review — mathlib PR #13057.
previews.label: «elliptic-divisibility-sequence»
previews.title: Theorem 7.24

previews.html:
The proof minimises a tau-functional, built from the Ruzsa distance (Definition 10.6), \tau[X_1 \,;\, X_2] \;\coloneqq\; d[X_1 \,;\, X_2] + \eta\bigl(d[X^0_1 \,;\, X_1] + d[X^0_2 \,;\, X_2]\bigr), with \eta = 1/9, over all pairs of G-valued random variables (X_1, X_2). A tau-minimiser exists by compactness (the group is finite); the crux is showing that if d[X_1 \,;\, X_2] > 0 at a minimiser then \tau can be strictly decreased — a contradiction.

The decreasing construction combines four independently chosen copies of the minimiser, forms four sums, and bounds their combined entropy using the entropic Ruzsa triangle inequality (Lemma 10.7) together with a fibring argument. The bound forces d[X_1 \,;\, X_2] = 0 at any minimiser.

From d[X_1 \,;\, X_2] = 0, the 100% theorem produces a subgroup H and a uniform U_H with d[X_1 \,;\, U_H] = d[X_2 \,;\, U_H] = 0. Transferring back via the tau-functional bound and the Ruzsa triangle inequality (Lemma 10.7) gives the stated 11 d[X^0_1 \,;\, X^0_2] bound.

The proof is independent of the Plünnecke–Ruzsa and small-doubling results in the deterministic setting above, but the underlying Ruzsa calculus — in particular the symmetry and triangle inequality of Ruzsa distance — is the same conceptual machinery (Theorem 10.4, Lemma 10.3). Formalised in pfr (sorry-free).
previews.label: «entropic-pfr»
previews.title: Theorem 10.8

previews.html: (Entropic PFR conjecture / Marton's conjecture, entropic form.) Let G be an elementary abelian 2-group (i.e. G \cong \mathbb{F}_2^n for some n) and let X^0_1, X^0_2 be G-valued random variables. Then there exists a subgroup H \le G and a random variable U_H uniformly distributed on H such that d[X^0_1 \,;\, U_H] + d[X^0_2 \,;\, U_H] \;\le\; 11 \cdot d[X^0_1 \,;\, X^0_2]. In the improved form (Liao), the constant 11 may be replaced by 10. Formalised in pfr (sorry-free).
previews.label: «entropic-pfr»
previews.title: Theorem 10.8

previews.html:
Work with independent copies X', Y', Z' of X, Y, Z placed on a common probability space. Unfolding the Ruzsa distance (Definition 10.6) of each pair and using the submodularity of entropy, H[X' - Z'] \;\le\; H[X' - Y'] + H[Y' - Z'] - H[Y']. Expanding d[X';Z'], d[X';Y'], d[Y';Z'] in terms of entropy and rearranging gives the triangle inequality.

This is the entropy-theoretic analogue of the set-combinatorial Ruzsa triangle inequality (Lemma 10.3): in the deterministic set setting, replacing H by \log-cardinality recovers |A - C| \cdot |B| \le |A - B| \cdot |B - C|. Formalised in pfr (sorry-free).
previews.label: «entropic-ruzsa-triangle»
previews.title: Lemma 10.7

previews.html: (Entropic Ruzsa triangle inequality.) For G-valued random variables X, Y, Z on respective probability spaces, d[X \,;\, Z] \;\le\; d[X \,;\, Y] + d[Y \,;\, Z]. Formalised in pfr (sorry-free).
previews.label: «entropic-ruzsa-triangle»
previews.title: Lemma 10.7

previews.html: The multiplicative group of \mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z} is cyclic of order p - 1. By Fermat's little theorem (Theorem 1.11) every nonzero a satisfies a^{p-1} = 1, so a^{(p-1)/2} = \pm 1. Picking a generator g, write a = g^k; then a^{(p-1)/2} = 1 iff (p-1) \mid k(p-1)/2 iff k is even, which is exactly the condition for a to be a square. The resulting value a^{(p-1)/2} \in \{\pm 1\} is precisely the Legendre symbol \left(\frac{a}{p}\right) (Definition 1.18), which records whether the nonzero a is a quadratic residue.
previews.label: «euler-criterion»
previews.title: Theorem 1.19

previews.html: (Euler's criterion.) For an odd prime p and a nonzero residue a \in \mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}, the element a is a square if and only if a^{(p-1)/2} = 1. Consequently \left(\frac{a}{p}\right) \equiv a^{(p-1)/2} \pmod{p}.
previews.label: «euler-criterion»
previews.title: Theorem 1.19

previews.html: For a weakly multiplicative arithmetic function f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{C} with f(1) = 1, if the L-series L(f, s) = \sum_{n \ge 1} f(n) n^{-s} (Definition 2.4) converges absolutely at s, then L(f,s) = \prod_{p} \sum_{e \ge 0} f(p^e)\, p^{-es}. When f is completely multiplicative the local factor simplifies to (1 - f(p)\,p^{-s})^{-1}. This is the general analytic incarnation of unique factorisation (Theorem 1.4): expanding the product and grouping by n = \prod_p p^{e_p} reproduces each Dirichlet coefficient f(n) n^{-s} exactly once. The Euler products for \zeta (Theorem 2.2) and for Dirichlet L-functions (Theorem 2.6) are the completely-multiplicative special cases. Formalised in EulerProducts (sorry-free).
previews.label: «euler-product-general»
previews.title: Definition 2.14

previews.html: The units of \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z} form a group of order \varphi(n) (Definition 1.5). A residue a coprime to n is such a unit, and by Lagrange's theorem its order divides the group order, so a^{\,\varphi(n)} = 1 in \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}, i.e. a^{\,\varphi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}. Fermat's little theorem (Theorem 1.11) is the special case n = p, where \varphi(p) = p - 1.
previews.label: «euler-theorem»
previews.title: Theorem 1.12

previews.html: (Euler's theorem.) If a is coprime to n, then a^{\,\varphi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}. Equivalently, every unit u of \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z} satisfies u^{\,\varphi(n)} = 1.
previews.label: «euler-theorem»
previews.title: Theorem 1.12

previews.html:
For a positive integer n, the Farey sequence F_n is the set of reduced fractions a/b \in [0,1] with denominator b \le n, listed in increasing order. Two consecutive terms \tfrac{a}{b} < \tfrac{c}{d} of F_n are Farey neighbours, characterised by the determinant identity bc - ad \;=\; 1, and their mediant \tfrac{a+c}{b+d} is the fraction with smallest denominator strictly between them. The number of terms is |F_n| \;=\; 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{n} \varphi(k), counting the reduced fractions of each denominator by Euler's totient (Definition 1.5).

PR #40246 develops Farey sequences, the mediant operation, and the neighbour theorem (the bc - ad = 1 characterisation of adjacency), the combinatorial backbone of the Stern–Brocot tree and of best-approximation arguments. In review — mathlib PR #40246.
previews.label: «farey-sequence»
previews.title: Definition 1.26

previews.html: Fermat's Last Theorem is the statement: for every natural number n \ge 3, there are no positive natural numbers a, b, c satisfying a^n + b^n = c^n. Equivalently, Definition 8.2 holds for every n \ge 3.
previews.label: «fermat-last-theorem»
previews.title: Definition 8.3

previews.html: The nonzero residues form a group of order p - 1 under multiplication. By Lagrange's theorem the order of any element divides p - 1, so raising any nonzero a to the power p - 1 yields the identity.
previews.label: «fermat-little-units»
previews.title: Theorem 1.11

previews.html: For a prime p and a nonzero residue a \in \mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}, a^{\,p-1} = 1.
previews.label: «fermat-little-units»
previews.title: Theorem 1.11

previews.html: The ring \mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z} is a field with p elements, so its multiplicative group has order p - 1. If a \ne 0 then a^{\,p-1} = 1 (Theorem 1.11), and multiplying by a gives a^{\,p} = a; the case a = 0 is immediate.
previews.label: «fermat-little»
previews.title: Theorem 1.10

previews.html: (Fermat's little theorem.) For a prime p and any residue a \in \mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}, a^{\,p} = a.
previews.label: «fermat-little»
previews.title: Theorem 1.10

previews.html: For negative k, the space is zero (the slash-invariance and holomorphicity force vanishing). For even non-negative k, finite-dimensionality follows directly from the dimension formula (Theorem 6.20). For odd k, the element -1 \in \mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z}) acts on any modular form of odd weight by -1, so f = -f, hence f = 0, and the space is again zero.
previews.label: «finite-dimensional-modular-forms»
previews.title: Theorem 6.24

previews.html: For every weight k \in \mathbb{Z}, the space M_k(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})) is finite-dimensional over \mathbb{C}.
previews.label: «finite-dimensional-modular-forms»
previews.title: Theorem 6.24

previews.html: The unique irregular index of 37 is 2k = 32, so k = 16 is even and 37 \equiv 1 \pmod 4; thus Vandiver's Theorem III applies, reducing \mathrm{FLT}_{37} to a first case (37 \nmid abc) and a second case (37 \mid abc). Case I runs the Mirimanoff polynomials \varphi_n(t) = \sum_j j^{n-1} t^j against Stickelberger's annihilator (Theorem 8.23) and the Bernoulli parity hypothesis. Case II is the Washington–Lehmer–Vandiver descent on \sigma-stable real cyclotomic data, which requires 37 \nmid h^+; this last fact is proved unconditionally here from Sinnott's index formula (Theorem 8.21) and a direct congruence check on B_{32} \bmod 37^2. The two cases are reduced to explicit cyclotomic-integer hypotheses at p = 37 (the Mirimanoff–Bernoulli conclusion and the case-II descent lemma) whose discharge is the remaining work.
previews.label: «flt-37»
previews.title: Theorem 8.28

previews.html: (Fermat's Last Theorem for the exponent 37, the smallest irregular prime.) The Fermat property (Definition 8.2) holds at p = 37: a^{37} + b^{37} = c^{37} has no solution in positive integers. The argument uses Vandiver's parity criterion (Theorem III) rather than regularity, since 37 is irregular. Formalised in flt-regular-bernoulli (in progress).
previews.label: «flt-37»
previews.title: Theorem 8.28

previews.html: For a natural number n, the Fermat property for exponent n is the assertion that the equation a^n + b^n = c^n has no solution in positive natural numbers. Formally, \texttt{FermatLastTheoremFor}\; n is the Fermat equation with exponent n over \mathbb{N} (Definition 8.1), i.e. \texttt{FermatLastTheoremWith}\; \mathbb{N}\; n.
previews.label: «flt-for»
previews.title: Definition 8.2

previews.html: A solution a^4 + b^4 = c^4 with a, b, c \ne 0 would give a^4 + b^4 = (c^2)^2, contradicting Theorem 8.5. Hence no such solution exists.
previews.label: «flt-four»
previews.title: Theorem 8.6

previews.html: Fermat's Last Theorem holds for exponent 4: there are no positive natural numbers a, b, c satisfying a^4 + b^4 = c^4.
previews.label: «flt-four»
previews.title: Theorem 8.6

previews.html: Reducing to a prime exponent p \ge 5 (Theorem 8.4, Theorem 8.6, Theorem 8.7), a counterexample produces a Frey package and its Frey curve (Definition 8.29). The attached mod p representation \bar\rho is either reducible or irreducible; but Mazur's theorem (Theorem 8.31) excludes the former and the Wiles–Taylor–Wiles modularity theorem (Theorem 8.32) the latter. This contradiction shows no Frey package exists, hence Fermat's Last Theorem holds. In the Imperial formalisation the top result Wiles_Taylor_Wiles is assembled from these two inputs, which remain to be discharged.
previews.label: «flt-frey-reduction»
previews.title: Theorem 8.33

previews.html: (Reduction of Fermat's Last Theorem to modularity.) Definition 8.3 holds. Equivalently, there is no Frey package (Definition 8.29): a counterexample to Fermat's Last Theorem would yield a Frey curve whose mod p Galois representation is simultaneously not reducible and not irreducible, which is impossible. Formalised in FLT (in progress; the top-level statement is reduced to the Mazur and Wiles inputs).
previews.label: «flt-frey-reduction»
previews.title: Theorem 8.33

previews.html: Every integer n \ge 3 either is divisible by 4 or has an odd prime divisor p. In the first case, a solution a^n + b^n = c^n would yield a solution (a^{n/4})^4 + (b^{n/4})^4 = (c^{n/4})^4, reducing to Theorem 8.6. In the second case, write n = p \cdot k; then (a^k)^p + (b^k)^p = (c^k)^p is a solution at the prime exponent p. In both cases the assumed result for the smaller exponent applies via the monotonicity of the Fermat property under divisibility.
previews.label: «flt-odd-primes-suffice»
previews.title: Theorem 8.4

previews.html: It suffices to prove Fermat's Last Theorem for odd prime exponents: if Definition 8.2 holds for every odd prime p, then Definition 8.3 holds.
previews.label: «flt-odd-primes-suffice»
previews.title: Theorem 8.4

previews.html: In \mathcal{O}_K = \mathbb{Z}[\zeta_p] (Theorem 8.12), a solution factors as (z)^p = \prod_{i=0}^{p-1}(x + \zeta_p^i y). The ideals (x + \zeta_p^i y) are pairwise coprime, so unique factorisation in the Dedekind domain \mathcal{O}_K makes each (x + \zeta_p^i y) = \mathfrak{a}_i^p for some ideal \mathfrak{a}_i. Since [\mathfrak{a}_i]^p is trivial in the class group (Definition 3.6) and p \nmid \#\mathrm{Cl}(\mathcal{O}_K), each \mathfrak{a}_i is principal, giving x + \zeta_p^i y = u_i \alpha_i^p with u_i a unit. Comparing this with its complex conjugate modulo p and using that \alpha_i^p is congruent to a rational integer modulo p yields a congruence among 1, \zeta_p, \zeta_p^{2k}, \zeta_p^{2k-1} whose coefficients are forced to be divisible by p, contradicting p \nmid xy.
previews.label: «flt-regular-case-one»
previews.title: Theorem 8.15

previews.html: (Case I.) Let p be an odd regular prime (Definition 8.11). Then x^p + y^p = z^p has no solution in integers x, y, z with p \nmid xyz. Formalised in flt-regular (sorry-free).
previews.label: «flt-regular-case-one»
previews.title: Theorem 8.15

previews.html: This is the harder case, treated by infinite descent on the power of \lambda_p = 1 - \zeta_p dividing z. Generalising to the equation x^p + y^p + \varepsilon\,(1 - \zeta_p)^{pn} z^p = 0 over \mathcal{O}_K with \varepsilon a unit, the factorisation and coprimality analysis produces ideals that are p-th powers; principality (from regularity) yields a unit relation to which Kummer's Lemma (Theorem 8.14) applies, extracting p-th roots and a new solution with a strictly smaller exponent n. No minimal solution can exist, so there are none.
previews.label: «flt-regular-case-two»
previews.title: Theorem 8.16

previews.html: (Case II.) Let p be an odd regular prime (Definition 8.11). Then x^p + y^p = z^p has no solution in integers x, y, z with p \mid xyz. Formalised in flt-regular (sorry-free).
previews.label: «flt-regular-case-two»
previews.title: Theorem 8.16

previews.html: Any integer solution falls into one of two cases according to whether p \mid xyz. The case p \nmid xyz is excluded by Case I (Theorem 8.15) and the case p \mid xyz by Case II (Theorem 8.16). Together with the reduction of Fermat's Last Theorem to odd prime exponents (Theorem 8.4), this proves Definition 8.3 for every regular prime exponent.
previews.label: «flt-regular»
previews.title: Theorem 8.17

previews.html: (Fermat's Last Theorem for regular primes.) Let p be an odd regular prime (Definition 8.11). Then the Fermat property (Definition 8.2) holds at the exponent p: there are no positive integers x, y, z with x^p + y^p = z^p. Formalised in flt-regular (sorry-free).
previews.label: «flt-regular»
previews.title: Theorem 8.17

previews.html:
This establishes the Fermat property (Definition 8.2) at the exponent 3. The argument uses infinite descent in the ring \mathbb{Z}[\zeta_3] of Eisenstein integers — the ring of integers of the cyclotomic field \mathbb{Q}(\zeta_3) (Definition 3.14), where \zeta_3 is a primitive cube root of unity. One first handles Case 1 (when 3 \nmid abc): the factorisation a^3 + b^3 = (a + b)(a + \zeta_3 b)(a + \zeta_3^2 b) in \mathbb{Z}[\zeta_3] leads to a contradiction modulo 9, since the three factors are pairwise coprime and their product is a cube, forcing each to be a unit times a cube, but no Eisenstein integer is simultaneously a cube and congruent to the required residue modulo 9.

For Case 2 (when exactly one of a, b, c is divisible by 3, which is 3 \mid c after possible relabelling), one reduces to the generalised equation a^3 + b^3 = u \cdot c^3 where u is a unit of \mathbb{Z}[\zeta_3]. The same factorisation, combined with unique factorisation in \mathbb{Z}[\zeta_3], shows that each factor is (up to a unit) a perfect cube. Extracting cube roots yields a new triple (a', b', c') with a'^3 + b'^3 = u' c'^3 and |c'| < |c|. Since |c| cannot decrease indefinitely among positive integers, no minimal solution exists, and the equation has no solutions.
previews.label: «flt-three»
previews.title: Theorem 8.7

previews.html: Fermat's Last Theorem holds for exponent 3: there are no positive natural numbers a, b, c satisfying a^3 + b^3 = c^3.
previews.label: «flt-three»
previews.title: Theorem 8.7

previews.html:
Let R be a semiring and n a natural number. We say the Fermat equation with exponent n$* holds over $R$ if the only solutions to a^n + b^n = c^n, \quad a, b, c \in R are those in which at least one of a, b, c is zero. This is denoted \texttt{FermatLastTheoremWith}\; R\; n.

Note that the statement can fail for small or continuous rings: \texttt{FermatLastTheoremWith}\; \mathbb{N}\; 2 is false (3^2 + 4^2 = 5^2), and \texttt{FermatLastTheoremWith}\; \mathbb{R}\; 3 is false (1^3 + 1^3 = (2^{1/3})^3).
previews.label: «flt-with»
previews.title: Definition 8.1

previews.html: By Euler's four-square identity, the sums of four squares are closed under multiplication — the four-square analogue of the norm-multiplicativity that drives the two-square theorem (Theorem 1.23) — so it suffices to treat n = 0, 1 and each prime p. For an odd prime p, the same kind of counting of quadratic residues that underlies the two-square case produces, by pigeonhole, x, y with x^2 + y^2 + 1 \equiv 0 \pmod p, so some multiple mp with 1 \le m < p is a sum of four squares. A descent step then shows the least such m must be 1: if m > 1 one constructs, from a representation of mp, a representation of m'p with 0 < m' < m, contradicting minimality. Hence p itself is a sum of four squares.
previews.label: «four-square»
previews.title: Theorem 1.24

previews.html: (Lagrange's four-square theorem.) Every natural number n is a sum of four squares: there exist integers a, b, c, d with n = a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + d^2.
previews.label: «four-square»
previews.title: Theorem 1.24

previews.html: A Frey package (a, b, c, p) is a triple of pairwise-coprime nonzero integers with a \equiv 3 \pmod 4, b \equiv 0 \pmod 2, together with a prime p \ge 5, such that a^p + b^p = c^p. The associated Frey curve (Hellegouarch–Frey) is the elliptic curve (Definition 7.3) over \mathbb{Q} given by the Weierstrass equation (Definition 7.1) E : \quad Y^2 = X\,(X - a^p)(X + b^p). A counterexample to Fermat's Last Theorem yields a Frey package, hence a Frey curve. Formalised in FLT (sorry-free).
previews.label: «frey-curve»
previews.title: Definition 8.29

previews.html: Let R be a commutative ring of characteristic exponent p (so p is either 1 or a prime, and x^p = 0 \Rightarrow x = 0). The Frobenius endomorphism is the ring homomorphism \varphi \colon R \to R, \quad x \mapsto x^p. It is a ring homomorphism because in characteristic p, the binomial theorem gives (x+y)^p = x^p + y^p.
previews.label: «frobenius-endomorphism»
previews.title: Definition 4.7

previews.html: For an elliptic curve E (Definition 7.3) over \mathbb{F}_q, the Frobenius endomorphism \pi : E \to E is the isogeny acting on coordinates by the q-power map: on affine points \pi(x,y) = (x^q, y^q). Its degree is q (Silverman III.4.6), and \ker(\pi - 1) = E(\mathbb{F}_q) as sets. The trace of Frobenius is t = q + 1 - \#E(\mathbb{F}_q), so \#E(\mathbb{F}_q) = q + 1 - t (Definition 7.17).
previews.label: «frobenius-isogeny»
previews.title: Definition 7.18

previews.html: By strong induction on n. If n = 1 the product is empty and equals 1. Otherwise n has a smallest prime factor p, and n/p < n is recovered from its factorization by the inductive hypothesis. Multiplying by p and noting that the valuation of n at each prime q is that of n/p plus [q = p] (Definition 1.2) gives the displayed product for n.
previews.label: «fta-existence»
previews.title: Theorem 1.3

previews.html: (Fundamental theorem of arithmetic — existence.) Every positive integer n is recovered from its factorization as the product over primes n = \prod_{p} p^{\,v_p(n)}, a finite product since only finitely many exponents v_p(n) are nonzero.
previews.label: «fta-existence»
previews.title: Theorem 1.3

previews.html: If m and n are positive with v_p(m) = v_p(n) for all primes p, then reconstructing each side as a product of prime powers (Theorem 1.3) gives m = \prod_p p^{\,v_p(m)} = \prod_p p^{\,v_p(n)} = n. Phrased multiplicatively, two factorizations of the same number have, for each prime, the same number of occurrences, hence are permutations of one another.
previews.label: «fta-uniqueness»
previews.title: Theorem 1.4

previews.html: (Fundamental theorem of arithmetic — uniqueness.) A positive integer is determined by its factorization: if two positive integers have the same exponent v_p(\cdot) at every prime p, they are equal. Equivalently, the prime factorization of every positive integer is unique up to the order of the factors.
previews.label: «fta-uniqueness»
previews.title: Theorem 1.4

previews.html: By unique factorisation of ideals (Theorem 3.5), \mathfrak{p} S factors as \prod_i \mathfrak{P}_i^{e_i}, where each ramification index e_i is exactly the \mathfrak{P}_i-adic valuation (Definition 5.1) of \mathfrak{p} — the local measure of how \mathfrak{p} ramifies at \mathfrak{P}_i. The Chinese remainder theorem (for ideals) gives a ring isomorphism S / \mathfrak{p} S \;\cong\; \prod_i S / \mathfrak{P}_i^{e_i}. Each factor S/\mathfrak{P}_i^{e_i} has R/\mathfrak{p}-dimension e_i f_i, as follows from the filtration by powers of \mathfrak{P}_i and the identification of each successive quotient with the residue field S/\mathfrak{P}_i. Summing over i gives [S/\mathfrak{p}S : R/\mathfrak{p}] = \sum_i e_i f_i. Comparing with the K-dimension of L via the flatness of L \cong K \otimes_R S yields \sum_i e_i f_i = [L:K].
previews.label: «fundamental-identity»
previews.title: Theorem 3.13

previews.html: (Fundamental identity of ramification.) Let L/K be a finite extension of number fields (or, more generally, let S/R be a finite extension of Dedekind domains with fraction fields L/K). For any nonzero prime \mathfrak{p} of R, summing the products of ramification and inertia indices over all primes \mathfrak{P} of S lying over \mathfrak{p} gives \sum_{\mathfrak{P} \mid \mathfrak{p}} e(\mathfrak{P}|\mathfrak{p})\, f(\mathfrak{P}|\mathfrak{p}) = [L : K].
previews.label: «fundamental-identity»
previews.title: Theorem 3.13

previews.html: By the primitive element theorem, L = K(\alpha) for some \alpha. A K-automorphism of L is determined by where it sends \alpha, and the image must be a root of \mathrm{minpoly}_K(\alpha). Because L/K is Galois (Definition 4.1) it is separable and normal: separability makes the minimal polynomial have distinct roots, and normality forces all those roots to lie in L. The count of such automorphisms therefore equals the degree of the minimal polynomial, which equals [L : K].
previews.label: «galois-card-aut»
previews.title: Theorem 4.2

previews.html: Let L/K be a finite Galois extension. Then |\mathrm{Gal}(L/K)| = [L : K].
previews.label: «galois-card-aut»
previews.title: Theorem 4.2

previews.html: The maps in both directions are inverse order-reversing bijections. The map M \mapsto \mathrm{Gal}(L/M) is well-defined since L/M is again Galois. For the key identity L^{\mathrm{Gal}(L/M)} = M: by Theorem 4.2, |\mathrm{Gal}(L/M)| = [L:M], and the fixed field of a group of automorphisms has degree over K equal to [G : \mathrm{Gal}(L/M)] by the same count, forcing L^{\mathrm{Gal}(L/M)} = M. The reverse direction H \mapsto L^H works because L/L^H is Galois with group H, so applying the first direction recovers H.
previews.label: «galois-correspondence»
previews.title: Theorem 4.3

previews.html: (Fundamental theorem of Galois theory.) Let L/K be a finite Galois extension with G = \mathrm{Gal}(L/K). There is an order-reversing bijection \left\{\text{intermediate fields } K \subseteq M \subseteq L\right\} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \left\{\text{subgroups of } G\right\} sending an intermediate field M to the fixing subgroup \mathrm{Gal}(L/M), and a subgroup H \le G to the fixed field L^H. Under this bijection, [L : M] = |H| and [M : K] = [G : H].
previews.label: «galois-correspondence»
previews.title: Theorem 4.3

previews.html: In a Galois extension all primes over \mathfrak{p} are conjugate under G (Theorem 4.3), so they share a common ramification index e (Definition 3.11) and a common inertia degree f (Definition 3.12). The general fundamental identity \sum_i e_i f_i = [L:K] over all primes \mathfrak{P}_i over \mathfrak{p} (Theorem 3.13) therefore collapses, with r equal summands, to r \cdot e \cdot f = [L:K] = |G|.
previews.label: «galois-efr-identity»
previews.title: Theorem 4.6

previews.html: Let B/A be a Galois extension of Dedekind domains with Galois group G, and let \mathfrak{p} be a nonzero prime of A. Then r \cdot e \cdot f \;=\; |G|, where r is the number of primes of B lying over \mathfrak{p}, e is their common ramification index e(\mathfrak{P}/\mathfrak{p}), and f is their common inertia degree f(\mathfrak{P}/\mathfrak{p}).
previews.label: «galois-efr-identity»
previews.title: Theorem 4.6

previews.html:
(The Galois group is generated by inertia.) Let L/K be a finite Galois extension of number fields (Definition 4.1) with group G = \mathrm{Gal}(L/K), and suppose K = \mathbb{Q} (or, more generally, that K has no unramified extensions inside L). Then G is generated by the inertia subgroups I(\mathfrak{P}) (Definition 4.4) attached to the primes \mathfrak{P} of L that ramify over K: G \;=\; \bigl\langle\, I(\mathfrak{P}) \;:\; \mathfrak{P} \text{ ramified in } L/\mathbb{Q} \,\bigr\rangle. Equivalently, the maximal subextension of L/\mathbb{Q} unramified at every finite prime is \mathbb{Q} itself — Minkowski's theorem that \mathbb{Q} admits no nontrivial everywhere-unramified extension.

The PR establishes that the inertia subgroups generate G, the group-theoretic counterpart of the absence of unramified extensions of \mathbb{Q}; the subgroup generated by all inertia groups fixes precisely the maximal unramified subextension. In review — mathlib PR #33992.
previews.label: «galois-generated-by-inertia»
previews.title: Theorem 4.25

previews.html: Expanding the product \tau(\chi)\tau(\bar\chi) = \sum_{a,b} \chi(a)\bar\chi(b)\, e^{2\pi i (a+b)/p} and substituting a = tb on the terms with b \ne 0, the inner sum over b vanishes unless t = -1, leaving \chi(-1)\,p. Since \overline{\tau(\chi)} = \chi(-1)\tau(\bar\chi), the absolute-value identity |\tau(\chi)|^2 = p follows.
previews.label: «gauss-sum»
previews.title: Definition 8.10

previews.html: For a Dirichlet character \chi modulo p (Definition 2.3) and the additive character a \mapsto e^{2\pi i a/p}, the Gauss sum is \tau(\chi) = \sum_{a \in \mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}} \chi(a)\, e^{2\pi i a / p} \in \mathbb{C}. For non-trivial \chi it satisfies \tau(\chi)\,\tau(\bar\chi) = \chi(-1)\,p and |\tau(\chi)|^2 = p. Formalised in flt-regular-bernoulli (sorry-free).
previews.label: «gauss-sum»
previews.title: Definition 8.10

previews.html: The n-th convergent C_n of a generalised continued fraction (Definition 9.13) is the rational number obtained by truncating the expansion after the n-th partial denominator. Convergents are computed by the recurrence A_{-1} = 1,\quad A_0 = h,\quad A_n = b_{n-1}A_{n-1} + a_{n-1}A_{n-2} B_{-1} = 0,\quad B_0 = 1,\quad B_n = b_{n-1}B_{n-1} + a_{n-1}B_{n-2} C_n = A_n / B_n, where A_n and B_n are the numerator and denominator continuants.
previews.label: «gcf-convergents»
previews.title: Definition 9.14

previews.html:
(Gelfond–Schneider theorem, Hilbert's seventh problem.) Let \alpha, \beta be algebraic numbers with \alpha \ne 0, \alpha \ne 1, and \beta \notin \mathbb{Q}. Then any value of \alpha^{\beta} \;=\; \exp(\beta \log \alpha) is transcendental. In particular 2^{\sqrt{2}} (the Gelfond–Schneider constant) and e^{\pi} = (-1)^{-i} are transcendental.

The proof is the auxiliary-function method: assuming \alpha^\beta algebraic, one builds an auxiliary polynomial in \alpha^x and \alpha^{\beta x} that vanishes to high order at many integer points, then derives a contradiction from an arithmetic lower bound on its nonzero derivatives versus an analytic upper bound — the same tension between good rational/algebraic approximation and transcendence that drives Liouville's theorem (Theorem 9.21), with the pigeonhole construction of the auxiliary function resting on Diophantine approximation (Theorem 9.17). PR #35735 formalises the analytic core: the non-vanishing and lower bounds for the Gelfond–Schneider auxiliary function (the companion PRs #33050, #35743, #35744 supply further analytic lemmas). In review — mathlib PR #35735.
previews.label: «gelfond-schneider»
previews.title: Theorem 9.24

previews.html: For a Dirichlet character \chi modulo p (Definition 2.3), the generalised Bernoulli numbers B_{n,\chi} are defined by \sum_{a=1}^{p} \chi(a)\, \frac{t e^{at}}{e^{pt} - 1} = \sum_{n \ge 0} B_{n,\chi} \frac{t^n}{n!}. They specialise to the classical Bernoulli numbers (Definition 8.8) at the trivial character and encode the special values of Dirichlet L-functions: L(1 - n, \chi) = -B_{n,\chi}/n. Only the values B_{1,\chi^{-1}} for odd \chi enter the relative class-number formula.
previews.label: «generalised-bernoulli»
previews.title: Definition 8.9

previews.html: Write the Frobenius isogeny as \pi and consider the endomorphism r\pi - s for integers r, s. Because the degree map on \mathrm{End}(E) is a positive semi-definite quadratic form (Silverman III.6.3), one has \deg(r\pi - s) = qr^2 - tr \cdot s + s^2 \;\ge\; 0 \quad\text{for all } r, s \in \mathbb{Z}. Setting r = t and s = 2q shows the discriminant t^2 - 4q \le 0, i.e. t^2 \le 4q. The real bound |t| \le 2\sqrt{q} follows by taking square roots. The identification \deg(1-\pi) = \#E(\mathbb{F}_q) rests on \ker(1-\pi) = E(\mathbb{F}_q) and separability of 1-\pi (for q \ge 2), which gives \deg(1-\pi) = \#\ker(1-\pi) = \#E(\mathbb{F}_q).
previews.label: «hasse-bound»
previews.title: Theorem 7.19

previews.html: (Hasse's theorem.) For an elliptic curve E (Definition 7.3) over a finite field \mathbb{F}_q with trace of Frobenius t = q + 1 - \#E(\mathbb{F}_q) (Definition 7.18, Definition 7.17), \bigl|\,\#E(\mathbb{F}_q) - (q+1)\,\bigr| \;\le\; 2\sqrt{q}, equivalently t^2 \le 4q. Formalised in Hasse-Weil (in progress — the discriminant-bound and real-algebra steps are sorry-free; two geometric deferred witnesses remain: the quadratic-form non-negativity of the degree map on \mathrm{End}(E), and the identification of \#\ker(1-\pi) with \#E(\mathbb{F}_q)).
previews.label: «hasse-bound»
previews.title: Theorem 7.19

previews.html: The denominator factor (1-t)(1-qt)^{-1} comes from the degree-0 and degree-2 cohomology groups H^0 \cong K and H^2 \cong K(-1), whose Frobenius eigenvalues are 1 and q respectively. The numerator 1 - t_0 t + q t^2 is P_1(t) = \det(1 - t\cdot\mathrm{Fr}^*\mid H^1(E)), which has trace t_0 and determinant q (Weil pairing). The Hasse bound (Theorem 7.19) is equivalent to the roots of P_1 having absolute value q^{-1/2}, consistent with the rationality formula.
previews.label: «hasse-weil-zeta-rational-for-EC»
previews.title: Lemma 7.22

previews.html: For an elliptic curve E (Definition 7.3) over \mathbb{F}_q, the Hasse–Weil zeta function (Definition 7.20) is explicitly rational: Z(E/\mathbb{F}_q,\, t) \;=\; \frac{1 - t_0\, t + q\, t^2}{(1-t)(1-qt)} with t_0 = q + 1 - \#E(\mathbb{F}_q) the trace of Frobenius (Definition 7.18, Definition 7.17).
previews.label: «hasse-weil-zeta-rational-for-EC»
previews.title: Lemma 7.22

previews.html: For a smooth projective scheme X over \mathbb{F}_q, write N_n = \#X(\mathbb{F}_{q^n}) for the number of rational points over the degree-n extension. The Hasse–Weil zeta function of X is the formal power series Z(X/\mathbb{F}_q,\, t) \;=\; \exp\!\Bigl(\sum_{n \ge 1} \frac{N_n}{n}\, t^n\Bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Q}[\![t]\!]. For an elliptic curve E (Definition 7.3) with trace of Frobenius t_0 (Definition 7.18), the zeta function takes the rational form Z(E/\mathbb{F}_q,\, t) \;=\; \frac{1 - t_0\, t + q\, t^2}{(1-t)(1-qt)}.
previews.label: «hasse-weil-zeta»
previews.title: Definition 7.20

previews.html: Additivity and \mathbb{C}-linearity of each individual Hecke operator T(n) are immediate from the linearity of the slash action (Definition 6.3). The substantive content is multiplicativity: the composition T(D_1) \circ T(D_2) equals T(D_2 \cdot D_1) as maps on M_k. This holds because the double-coset product in the Hecke ring is defined precisely so that the slash sums compose correctly (Shimura, Proposition 3.30): the coset representatives of the product double coset are exactly the products of coset representatives, up to the \mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})-symmetry which merely relabels summands in f|_k D. Commutativity of the Hecke algebra then forces the image in \operatorname{End}(M_k) to be a commutative subalgebra.
previews.label: «hecke-algebra-action»
previews.title: Theorem 6.27

previews.html:
The Hecke algebra acts on modular forms. For each weight k, the assignment T \mapsto (f \mapsto f|_k T) defines a ring homomorphism \mathbb{T} \;\longrightarrow\; \operatorname{End}_{\mathbb{C}}(M_k(\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z}))) from the abstract \mathrm{GL}_2 Hecke algebra into the \mathbb{C}-linear endomorphisms of M_k. The analogous statement holds at level \Gamma_0(N): there is a ring homomorphism from the congruence Hecke ring R(\Gamma_0(N), \Delta_0(N)) to \operatorname{End}_{\mathbb{C}}(M_k(\Gamma_0(N))). Formalised in LeanModularForms on branch hecke-ring (sorry-free).

Depends on: Definition 6.26 Definition 6.5
previews.label: «hecke-algebra-action»
previews.title: Theorem 6.27

previews.html: Multiplicativity is transported from the Hecke ring: inside the commutative ring R(\Gamma_0(N), \Delta_0(N)) the ring-side elements D_n satisfy D_{mn} = D_m D_n for coprime m, n by pure commutative algebra (the multiplication table of Shimura 3.24). Character-space homomorphisms sending D_n to \chi(n)^{-1} T(n) on each Nebentypus subspace M_k(N,\chi), combined with the direct-sum decomposition M_k(\Gamma_1(N)) = \bigoplus_\chi M_k(N,\chi), pull the ring identity back to the operator identity. Commutativity is inherited from the commutativity of the Hecke ring, established via Shimura's anti-involution argument for the Atkin–Lehner-conjugated transpose.
previews.label: «hecke-operator-multiplicativity»
previews.title: Theorem 6.28

previews.html:
Classical multiplicativity and commutativity of the T(n). For m, n \ge 1 coprime to N, the Hecke operators satisfy T(mn) \;=\; T(m)\,T(n) \quad (\gcd(m,n) = 1), and for a prime p the prime-power recurrence holds. Moreover, T(m) and T(n) commute for all m, n coprime to N, and each T(n) commutes with every diamond operator \langle d \rangle. Formalised in LeanModularForms on branch hecke-ring (sorry-free).

Depends on: Definition 6.26
previews.label: «hecke-operator-multiplicativity»
previews.title: Theorem 6.28

previews.html:
Hecke operators T(n) on M_k(\Gamma_1(N)). Fix a positive integer N and weight k. For each positive integer n, the Hecke operator T(n) is a \mathbb{C}-linear endomorphism of M_k(\Gamma_1(N)). At a prime p \nmid N it is defined by the explicit coset-summed slash action T(p)\,f \;=\; \sum_{b=0}^{p-1} f\big|_k\begin{pmatrix}1 & b\\ 0 & p\end{pmatrix} \;+\; \langle p\rangle f\big|_k\begin{pmatrix}p & 0\\ 0 & 1\end{pmatrix}, where \langle p\rangle is the diamond operator (Definition 6.3). At a prime p \mid N only the upper-triangular sum survives (the diamond term is zero). Higher prime-power operators T(p^v) satisfy the three-term recurrence T(p^{v+2}) \;=\; T(p)\,T(p^{v+1}) - p^{k-1}\,\langle p\rangle\,T(p^{v}), and general T(n) is assembled multiplicatively from prime-power components. Formalised in LeanModularForms on branch hecke-ring (sorry-free).

Depends on: Definition 6.5 Definition 6.3
previews.label: «hecke-operator»
previews.title: Definition 6.26

previews.html: The proof is a p-adic Newton iteration. Define a sequence a_0 = a and a_{n+1} = a_n - F(a_n)/F'(a_n). Because \|F(a)\|_p < \|F'(a)\|_p^2, Newton's correction F(a_n)/F'(a_n) has norm strictly less than \|F'(a)\|_p, and the ultrametric inequality (Lemma 5.8) ensures that the sequence (a_n) is Cauchy. By completeness of \mathbb{Z}_p (Theorem 5.10), it converges to a limit z. Continuity of F and the norm estimate give F(z) = 0, and uniqueness follows because any two roots close to a coincide via the same norm estimate.
previews.label: «hensels-lemma»
previews.title: Theorem 5.13

previews.html:
(Hensel's lemma over \mathbb{Z}_p.) Let F \in \mathbb{Z}_p[X] be a polynomial and a \in \mathbb{Z}_p an approximate root satisfying

\|F(a)\|_p < \|F'(a)\|_p^2.

Then there exists a unique z \in \mathbb{Z}_p with

F(z) = 0, \quad \|z - a\|_p < \|F'(a)\|_p, \quad \|F'(z)\|_p = \|F'(a)\|_p.
previews.label: «hensels-lemma»
previews.title: Theorem 5.13

previews.html:
For h(G, \mathcal{O}_l^\times) = 1: filter \mathcal{O}_l^\times \supset 1 + \pi_k\mathcal{O}_l \supset \cdots by powers of a uniformiser. The successive subquotients are isomorphic (as G-modules) to \mathbb{F}_l^\times and to additive copies of \mathbb{F}_l, both of which have trivial Tate cohomology by a Serre approximation argument (normal basis theorem for l \cong k[G]). Since \mathcal{O}_l^\times / M is finite for the good open subgroup M of the approximation, the Herbrand multiplicativity for short exact sequences (Definition 4.19) gives h(\mathcal{O}_l^\times) = h(M) = 1.

For h(G, l^\times) = [l:k]: split l^\times \cong \mathcal{O}_l^\times \oplus \mathbb{Z} via the valuation, so h(l^\times) = h(\mathcal{O}_l^\times) \cdot h(\mathbb{Z}) = 1 \cdot [l:k] = [l:k].
previews.label: «herbrand-local-units»
previews.title: Theorem 4.20

previews.html: Let l/k be a cyclic extension of local fields (Definition 4.15) with G = \mathrm{Gal}(l/k) (Definition 4.1). Then: h(G, \mathcal{O}_l^\times) = 1 \quad\text{and}\quad h(G, l^\times) = [l:k]. Here \mathcal{O}_l^\times denotes the unit group of the ring of integers of l. Formalised in ClassFieldTheory (sorry-free).
previews.label: «herbrand-local-units»
previews.title: Theorem 4.20

previews.html: Let G be a finite cyclic group and M a \mathbb{Z}[G]-module. By the periodicity of Tate cohomology for cyclic groups, \widehat{H}^n(G,M) \cong \widehat{H}^{n+2}(G,M) for all n. The Herbrand quotient of M is h(G,M) = \frac{|\widehat{H}^0(G,M)|}{|\widehat{H}^{-1}(G,M)|} = \frac{|M^G / N_G M|}{|\ker(N_G)/I_G M|}, with the convention h(G,M) = 0 if either group is infinite. For the trivial G-module \mathbb{Z}, h(G, \mathbb{Z}) = |G|.
previews.label: «herbrand-quotient»
previews.title: Definition 4.19
